query: longitudinal studies on remission type and relapse risk in major depressive disorder over 4-year follow-up

retrieved_docs.title: Depression in Children -- Prognosis -- Curry et al investigated recovery and recurrence rates following treatment for adolescent MDD in the Treatment for Adolescents with Depression Study (TADS). The study reported that 88% of adolescents achieved recovery within 5 years; however, 46% experienced a recurrence. Risk factors for recurrence included greater baseline severity and residual symptoms after treatment. These findings highlight the chronic and recurrent nature of adolescent MDD and emphasize the need for long-term monitoring and sustained intervention. This study offers valuable insights into the course of adolescent depression and informs strategies to prevent relapse. [61]
retrieved_docs.content: Adolescents with chronic or recurrent depression are at increased risk for persistent mood disorders in adulthood. [47] In specialist psychiatry settings, patients with MDD have long-term recurrence rates between 50% and 64%. [56] Relapse rates are notably higher following partial remission (67.6%) compared to complete remission (15.18%). [57] Children and adolescents who experience recurrent or chronic depression that extends into adulthood are more likely to endure significant disability and impairment. [58]

retrieved_docs.title: Persistent Depressive Disorder -- History and Physical -- Diagnostic criteria for persistent depressive disorder
retrieved_docs.content: According to the DSM-5-TR, the following are the diagnostic criteria for persistent depressive disorder: A. Depressed mood most of the day, more days than not, for at least 2 years. B. At least 2 of the following 6 symptoms: Poor appetite or overeating Insomnia or hypersomnia Low energy or fatigue Low self-esteem Poor concentration or difficulty making decisions Feelings of hopelessness C. During a 2-year period (1 year for children or adolescents), the individual has never been without the symptoms in Criteria A and B for more than 2 months at a time. D. Criteria for a major depressive disorder may be continuously present for 2 years. E. There has never been a manic or hypomanic episode. F. The disturbance is not better explained by a schizophrenia spectrum or other psychotic disorder. G. The symptoms are not attributable to the physiological effects of a substance or another medical condition.

retrieved_docs.title: Persistent Depressive Disorder -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Persistent depressive disorder (PDD), as defined in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition (DSM-5), merges the previous diagnoses of dysthymic disorder and chronic major depressive disorder. This condition is marked by a depressed mood lasting most of the day, for more days than not, over at least 2 years in adults or 1 year in children and adolescents. PDD can begin early in life and often becomes chronic, with symptoms that persist or fluctuate but never completely resolve. Individuals with PDD face a heightened risk of suicidal thoughts and behaviors, with functional impairments that can be as severe as or more severe than those experienced in major depressive disorder.

retrieved_docs.title: Veteran and Military Mental Health Issues -- Prognosis -- Depression
retrieved_docs.content: MDD is variable, with less than half of cases resolving within 3 months of onset, compared to approximately 80% showing improvement within 12 months. MDDs falling under the severe specifier are associated with a higher risk of recurrence and can sometimes become a chronic condition requiring ongoing attention and maintenance. Studies have shown that patients who continued with pharmacotherapy for a minimum of 6 to 12 months following their first depressive episode experienced less risk of recurrence of symptoms than those who discontinued medication earlier (25% compared to 50%). Although remission is the ultimate goal, trials show that continued care and collaborative treatment programs reduce the prevalence and incidence of MDDs later in life. [88]

retrieved_docs.title: Late-Life Depression -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Depression was traditionally considered as a self-limiting and curable illness. After the depressive phase was over, patients were expected to revert to a pre-morbid level of normal functioning. Management was done on ‘as and when’ required basis. Long-term treatment was not encouraged. Without treatment, depression tends to assume a chronic course, be recurrent and associated with increased disability. [20] In comparison with other chronic diseases like diabetes, asthma, angina, and arthritis, depression impairs health status to a greater degree. [21] Co-morbidity with depression worsens the health status of people with chronic diseases. Recurrence is more common if symptoms of depression do not receive adequate treatment. Current guidelines recommend continuing antidepressants for 4 to 6 months after remission to prevent relapse. After improvement, 50% relapse within one year and most within two years. Those who had one episode of depression 50-85% will go on to have the second episode of depression. About 80 to 90% of those who had the second episode of depression will go on to have a third one. Around 90% with severe depression experience recurrence. [22] There is evidence that chronic depression is more familial, more refractory to treatment, and more impairing than episodic major depression. Poor prognostic factors can categorize as patient factors and illness factors:

retrieved_docs.title: Persistent Depressive Disorder -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Medication and psychotherapy combined may result in higher response rates and improved functioning.

retrieved_docs.title: Persistent Depressive Disorder -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Assess individuals for persistent depressive disorder based on history and physical examination. Identify the diagnostic criteria for persistent depressive disorder and recognize the clinical presentation of this disorder. Implement evidence-based treatments for individuals with persistent depressive disorder. Apply effective strategies to improve care coordination among interprofessional team members to facilitate positive outcomes for individuals with persistent depressive disorder. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Major Depressive Disorder -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Untreated depressive episodes in major depressive disorder can last from 6 to 12 months. About two-thirds of the individuals with MDD contemplate suicide, and about 10 to 15 percent commit suicide. MDD is a chronic, recurrent illness; the recurrence rate is about 50% after the first episode, 70% after the second episode, and 90% after the third episode. About 5 to 10 percent of the patients with MDD eventually develop bipolar disorder. [11] The prognosis of MDD is good in patients with mild episodes, the absence of psychotic symptoms, better treatment compliance, a strong support system, and good premorbid functioning. The prognosis is poor in the presence of a comorbid psychiatric disorder, personality disorder, multiple hospitalizations, and advanced age of onset.

retrieved_docs.title: Persistent Depressive Disorder -- History and Physical -- Diagnostic criteria for persistent depressive disorder
retrieved_docs.content: H. The symptoms cause clinically significant distress or impairment in social, occupational, or other important areas of functioning.

retrieved_docs.title: Seasonal Affective Disorder -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Relapse prevention: Patients who have experienced SAD in the past are at an increased risk of recurrence in subsequent years. Proactive measures such as continuing maintenance treatments, implementing lifestyle modifications, and practicing self-care strategies during high-risk seasons can help prevent relapse and maintain long-term wellness.

retrieved_docs.title: Late-Life Depression -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Patient factors are: 1. Family history of affective disorder 2. Pre-existing dysthymias 3. Comorbid anxiety disorder 4. Comorbid substance abuse 5. Low T3 level Illness factors are: 1. Previous depressive episode 2. Longer and severe index episode 3. Residual sub-syndromal depression 4. The first episode after the age of 60 5. Seasonal pattern

retrieved_docs.title: Bipolar Disorder -- Evaluation -- The presence of 5 or more of the following symptoms daily or nearly every day for a consecutive 2-week period that is a change from baseline or previous functioning:
retrieved_docs.content: Subjective report of depressed mood most of the day (or depressed mood observed by others) Anhedonia most of the day Significant weight loss when not dieting or weight gain or decrease or increase in appetite Insomnia or hypersomnia Psychomotor agitation or retardation Fatigue or loss of energy Feelings of worthlessness or excessive or inappropriate guilt Decreased concentration or indecisiveness Recurrent thoughts of death, recurrent suicidal ideation without a specific plan

retrieved_docs.title: Seasonal Affective Disorder -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The course of SAD varies, with symptoms typically improving on bright sunny days and sometimes persisting into summer. Recurrence rates from 1 winter to the next range from 50% to 70%, and the seasonal pattern of recurrent unipolar major depression tends not to be a long-term phenomenon in many patients. [34] Longitudinal studies indicate that less than half of patients continue to experience recurrent depression with a seasonal pattern over several years, with a significant proportion recovering without further episodes of major depression. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Persistent Depressive Disorder -- Pearls and Other Issues
retrieved_docs.content: Treatment success in persistent depressive disorder may be lower due to delays in diagnosing the disorder and starting treatment, patient hopelessness, and inadequate treatment duration.

retrieved_docs.title: Separation Anxiety Disorder -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: A longitudinal study surveying anxiety symptoms in 242 participants with a mean age of 10 years found that 56% had an elevated SCARED score at 1-year follow-up and 32% had elevated scores at 3-year follow-up. Eight percent of the participants in this study had a fluctuating course. [48] Most studies report that anxiety disorders tend to have a chronic and unremitting course if left untreated. [41] With treatment, childhood anxiety disorders are believed to have a good prognosis [43] , but long-term longitudinal data supporting this claim is limited. [49] A 4-year study evaluating adolescents and young adults with childhood anxiety disorders reported that only 21.7% of the patients were in stable remission, 48% relapsed, and 30% were "chronically ill" at the 4-year mark. The assigned treatment in the study (SSRI, CBT, SSRI plus CBT, or placebo) did not correlate with the likelihood of remission. [50]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2287
retrieved_docs.content: Major depressive disorder with a recurrent episode, major depressive disorder with a single episode, major depressive disorder with a single episode in full remission, atypical depression (اكتئاب أوبِيّ), and mood lability (التقلقل الوجداني) are all classified as members of the schizophrenia and psychotic disorders SMQ (الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ)). The SMQ itself is categorized under the broader construct of blunted affect (عاطفة متقلصة). This hierarchical organization indicates that these depressive conditions are considered within the spectrum of schizophrenia‑related and psychotic disorders, and that the entire group is associated with a reduction in emotional expressivity. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians appreciate the overlap between mood disturbances and psychotic features, and underscores the importance of assessing affective flattening when evaluating patients with these diagnoses.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 435
retrieved_docs.content: The classification “الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ)” is presented as a broad grouping that encompasses a variety of psychotic and affective conditions. Within this group are several specific entities: “ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة” (unspecified childhood psychosis, current or active), “هوس شَبَقِيّ” (schizotypal mania), and “عاطفة متقلصة” (blunted affect). In addition to primary psychotic disorders, the SMQ grouping also includes mood‑related diagnoses such as “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة” (major depressive disorder, recurrent episode), “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة” (major depressive disorder, single episode), “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة” (major depressive disorder, single episode in full remission), “اكتئاب أَوبِيّ” (atypical depression), and “التقلقل الوجداني” (affective lability). This reflects an integrated view in which depressive disorders and affective symptoms are considered part of the broader psychosis spectrum, especially when they may present with psychotic features. The entity “ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة” is also linked to the more general category “disorders usually diagnosed in infancy,” indicating that this form of childhood psychosis is typically identified early in life. Furthermore, “هوس شَبَقِيّ” is related to “ذُهان عضوي” (organic psychosis) through a broader‑relationship link, suggesting that schizotypal mania can be viewed as a subtype or manifestation of an organic psychotic process. Overall, the SMQ framework integrates early‑onset psychotic conditions, organic psychoses, specific manic presentations, and a range of depressive and affective disorders, highlighting the clinical overlap among psychotic and mood disturbances and underscoring the importance of considering these interrelationships in diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 962
retrieved_docs.content: The category “disorders usually diagnosed in infancy” has a broader relationship with ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة (unspecified childhood psychosis, current or active). This specific psychotic condition is a member of the larger classification الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) (psychosis and psychotic disorders). Within the الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) group, several mood‑related diagnoses are also listed as members: اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة (major depressive disorder, recurrent episode), اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة (major depressive disorder, single episode), اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة (major depressive disorder, single episode in full remission), اكتئاب أَوبِيّ (atypical depression), and the symptom التقلقل الوجداني (mood lability). Moreover, the entire الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) category is itself a member of عاطفة متقلصة (blunted affect). This network of relationships illustrates the overlap between early‑onset psychotic conditions, broader psychotic disorder classifications, depressive mood disorders, and affective symptoms such as blunted affect, emphasizing the need for integrated assessment and management in patient care.

Depressive disorders are organized under the category “depression excluding suicide and self‑harm” (الاكتئاب (باستثناء الانتحار وإيذاء النفس) (SMQ)) and include recurrent major depressive episode (اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة), single major depressive episode (اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة), single episode in full remission (اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة), endogenous depression (اكتئاب داخلي المنشأ), reactive depression (اكتئاب تفاعلي), melancholic depression (اكتئاب سوداوي), atypical depression (اكتئاب أَوبِيّ), as well as transient and persistent disorders of awakening (الاضطراب العابر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو المحافظة عليه, الاضطراب المستمر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو استمراره). retrieved_docs.content: Alcohol and drug rehabilitation/detoxification is linked to متلازمة الامتناع عن تعاطي الكحول (alcohol abstinence syndrome), which in turn includes الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) as a component of its clinical picture. الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) comprises a broad spectrum of psychotic and affective conditions. It contains ذُهان الطفولة غير المحدد، حالة راهنة أو نشطة (unspecified childhood schizophrenia, active or current state), reflecting that disorders usually diagnosed in infancy have a broader relationship with early‑onset psychotic presentations. Within the SMQ group are several depressive entities: اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة (major depressive disorder, recurrent episode), اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة (major depressive disorder, single episode), اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة (major depressive disorder, single episode in full remission), اكتئاب أَوبِيّ (obligatory depression), and التقلقل الوجداني (mood lability). Additionally, الذهان والاضطرابات الذهانية (SMQ) is associated with عاطفة متقلصة (blunted affect), indicating affective flattening as a characteristic feature of this syndrome cluster. Together, these relationships illustrate how substance‑use treatment, early‑life psychotic disorders, various depressive states, mood instability, and blunted affect are interconnected within the framework of the SMQ and alcohol abstinence syndrome, highlighting important considerations for comprehensive patient assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 220
retrieved_docs.content: The community defines “mental symptom” as a construct that includes the sub‑concepts “probleem; persoonlijkheid” and “فقد المتعة” (loss of pleasure) and is directly linked to “السلوك التدميري الذاتي” (self‑destructive behavior). Within the overarching category “الاكتئاب (باستثناء الانتحار وإيذاء النفس) (SMQ)” (depression excluding suicide and self‑harm), a wide spectrum of specific disorders and clinical presentations is enumerated: recurrent major depressive episode (“اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة”), single‑episode major depressive disorder (“اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة”), single episode in full remission (“اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة”), postpartum depression excluding psychosis (“الاكتئاب التالي للولادة (باستثناء الذُهان)”), obligatory depression (“اكتئاب أَوبِيّ”), reactive depression (“اكتئاب تفاعلي”), endogenous depression (“اكتئاب داخلي المنشأ”), melancholic depression (“اكتئاب سوداوي”), affective lability (“التقلقل الوجداني”), emotional poverty (“الفقر

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 192
retrieved_docs.content: **اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة** (single‑episode major depressive episode), **اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة في هدأة تامة** (single episode in full remission), **اضطراب تعاطي المواد الإدمانية** (substance‑use disorder), **اكتئاب أَوبِيّ** (atypical/obip depressive disorder), **اكتئاب تفاعلي** (reactive depression), **اكتئاب داخلي المنشأ** (endogenous depression), **اكتئاب سوداوي** (melancholic depression), **الاضطراب العابر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو المحافظة عليه** (transient disorder of awakening initiation/maintenance), **الاضطراب المستمر في بدء الاستيقاظ أو استمراره** (persistent disorder of awakening initiation/maintenance), and **الاعتياد** (tolerance). This SMQ is itself a member of **الاكتئاب التالي للولادة (باستثناء الذُهان)** (post‑partum depression excluding psychosis), situating these conditions within the broader peripartum mood‑disorder context. At the symptom level, the higher‑order concept **Mental symptom** includes child manifestations such as **probleem** (problem),

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 0 -- Community 21
retrieved_docs.content: developmental pathways. “Preadult mental disorders” classifies attention‑deficit conditions, situating ADHD within the spectrum of mental disorders occurring before adulthood. Both “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة راجعة” (recurrent major depressive episode) and “اضطراب اكتئابي أساسي، نوبة واحدة” (single‑episode major depressive episode) are members of “الاكتئاب (باستثناء الانتحار وإيذاء النفس) (SMQ)” (depression excluding suicide and self‑harm), placing major depressive episodes within the broader depression construct. Depression (excluding suicide and self‑harm) (“الاكتئاب (باستثناء الانتحار وإيذاء النفس) (SMQ)”) is described as an umbrella syndrome that comprises major depressive disorder, single‑episode remission, recurrent major depressive disorder, substance‑use disorder (“اضطراب تعاطي المواد الإدمانية”), obligatory depression (“اكتئاب أَوبِيّ”), reactive depression (“اكتئاب تفاعلي”), endogenous depression (“اكتئاب داخلي المنشأ”), melancholic depression (“اكتئاب سوداوي”), transient
